summary: Gruber analyzes the iPad's paradoxical position as a 'post-PC' device that still requires a PC for activation, updates, backups, and media syncing. He breaks the tethered-iTunes dependency into four discrete problems and argues Apple will solve them incrementally rather than all at once, starting with device activation and software updates. He contends that cloud-based backup of large data isn't technically feasible yet, but that Apple is inexcusably behind on syncing small data like calendars, contacts, and app state — functionality that Android users get for free but iOS users must pay $99/year for MobileMe to access. Drawing an analogy to Jason Fried's advice about bootstrapping, Gruber argues Apple will only get good at cloud syncing by actually doing it, piece by piece. He concludes that the real gap isn't eliminating iTunes entirely, but making lightweight cloud sync free and ubiquitous across iOS.
key_points: The iPad is a generation ahead on hardware and software but a generation behind on cloud services — a perfect synopsis of iOS's state in 2011
The iTunes tethering problem should be understood as four discrete issues: device activation, software updates, media syncing, and backup/restore — each requiring different solutions
Apple TV 2 already demonstrates the path forward for activation and updates, proving these dependencies can be eliminated
The real competitive gap isn't large-data cloud backup but free wireless syncing of small data — calendars, contacts, bookmarks, and app state that Android users get automatically
Apple's own iWork apps have exquisite design but antediluvian syncing, while Google Docs has no UI panache but invisible, effortless file access across devices
money_quotes: The iPad 2 epitomized how Apple seems to be a generation ahead of its competitors on the device side — both hardware and software — but a generation behind on the cloud side.
I think it's worth considering these as discrete problems, rather than thinking about this as a single 'Apple should eliminate the dependency on tethered syncing to desktop iTunes' issue.
The only reliable way to succeed at anything is to actually do it, repeatedly, with concentrated effort.

I think Apple is smart enough to realize that they're never going to just 'flip a switch' and replace the entirety of iTunes's desktop syncing with equally reliable over-the-air cloud syncing all at once.
Should wireless Safari bookmark syncing cost $99 a year?

The iPad versions of the iWork suite and GarageBand are exquisite apps — easily some of the best-designed user experiences for creative software ever made. But the process of getting, say, a slide deck created in Keynote on your iPad open in Keynote on your iMac is downright antediluvian.
Google Docs has none of the UI panache, but the syncing is invisible. You just open Google Docs, and there are your files.
The announcement I'd like to see is for iOS users to no longer need to pay for MobileMe to wirelessly sync calendars, contacts — and any other small bits of data from apps from the App Store.
key_insight: Apple's real cloud deficit isn't about eliminating the USB cable — it's about making lightweight data syncing free and invisible, the way Google already does for Android users.
